questions.text: A wheel of radius 1 m covers an angular displacement of 180o. Its linear dispacement is
questions.subject: Physics

This option incorrectly doubles the correct answer, which is π meters, not 2π meters.

questions.explanation: When a wheel of radius 1 meter covers an angular displacement of 180 degrees, the linear displacement can be calculated using s = θ * r. First, convert 180 degrees to radians: θ = 180 degrees * (π radians / 180 degrees) = π radians. Then, calculate the linear displacement: s = π radians * 1 meter = π meters. Therefore, the correct answer is 3.14m. Option B is incorrect as it doubles the correct answer. Option C is incorrect as it only provides the result in radians. Option D is incorrect as it miscalculates the linear displacement.S = rθ = 1 x π = πm = 3.14mθ = 180° θ = 180° x (π/180°) = π rad

Option C (2π/180) is incorrect because it is equivalent to π/90, not the correct conversion factor.
Option D (π/57.3) is incorrect because it is an approximate value of the conversion factor, not the precise value.

questions.text: When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity v and angular velocity ω is always
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is not 0 degrees. The correct angle is 90 degrees.
When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is not 180 degrees. The correct angle is 90 degrees.
When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is not 360 degrees. The correct angle is 90 degrees.

Correct! When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is always 90 degrees. This relationship is due to the perpendicular nature of these vectors in circular motion.

questions.explanation: When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector (v) and angular velocity vector (ω) is always 90 degrees, or π/2 radians. This is because the velocity vector is tangential to the circle at any point, pointing in the direction of motion, while the angular velocity vector is perpendicular to the plane of the circle and points along the axis of rotation. As a result, the angle between them is always a right angle. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because the angle between velocity and angular velocity in circular motion is not 0, 180, or 360 degrees. The correct angle is always 90 degrees due to the specific geometric relationship between these vectors in circular motion.

questions.text: A body moves with constant angular velocity on a circle. Magnitude of angular acceleration is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
This option is correct.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below. If a body moves with constant angular velocity on a circle, then its angular acceleration is zero. This is because acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time. In circular motion, the velocity of the body is constantly changing as it moves around the circle, but the magnitude of the velocity remains constant. Therefore, the angular acceleration of the body is zero. Mathematically, we can express this as: Angular acceleration (α) = dω/dt where ω is the angular velocity of the body. Since the angular velocity is constant, the derivative of ω with respect to time is zero, so the angular acceleration is also zero.

We can use this information to calculate the angular speed:Angular speed (ω) = (Angular displacement) / (Time)The angular displacement for one full rotation is 2π radians (corresponding to 360 degrees).Time = 24 hoursAngular speed:ω = (2π radians) / (24 hours) = π/12

questions.explanation: The following is the solution:To calculate the angular speed of the Earth's daily rotation in radians per hour, we need to know the time it takes for the Earth to complete one full rotation.The Earth completes one full rotation in approximately 24 hours. questions.text: Linear acceleration a = rα is zero when:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the angular acceleration (α) is 0 degrees, it means there is no change in the angular velocity of the object. In other words, the object is not undergoing any rotation. When α is 0 degrees, the linear acceleration (a) will also be 0, regardless of the value of the radius (r). This is because the object is not accelerating linearly if it is not rotating.

If the angular acceleration (α) is 180 degrees, it means there is a significant decrease in the angular velocity of the object. The object may be slowing down or decelerating. In this case, the linear acceleration (a) will depend on the value of the radius (r) and the magnitude of the angular acceleration (α). The linear acceleration will be non-zero and determined by the formula a = rα.

If the angular acceleration (α) is 360 degrees, it means there is no change in the angular velocity of the object, similar to option (a). Again, when α is 360 degrees, the linear acceleration (a) will be 0, regardless of the value of the radius (r).

If the angular acceleration (α) is 90 degrees, it means there is a significant increase in the angular velocity of the object. The object may be speeding up or accelerating. In this case, the linear acceleration (a) will depend on the value of the radius (r) and the magnitude of the angular acceleration (α). The linear acceleration will be non-zero and determined by the formula a = rα.

questions.explanation: Please note that the correct option is a) 0 degrees. When the angular acceleration (α) is 0 degrees, the object is not undergoing any rotation, and the linear acceleration (a) will be 0, regardless of the value of the radius (r).

questions.text: The curved flight of fighter planes at high speed requires a large:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that gravitational force is responsible for the curved flight of fighter planes. While gravitational force is always present, it is not the primary force involved in maintaining the curved trajectory of the planes.

This option incorrectly implies that the frictional force is the dominant force in curved flight. While friction can play a role, it is not the main force responsible for keeping the planes in their curved paths.

This option correctly identifies that the curved flight of fighter planes requires a large centripetal force. This force is directed towards the centre of the circular path, keeping the planes in their curved trajectory.

This option incorrectly describes centrifugal acceleration, which is not a force but an apparent outward acceleration in a rotating frame of reference. It is not the force responsible for maintaining the planes' curved flight.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option C: Centripetal force. Fighter planes in curved flight require a large centripetal force directed towards the centre of the circular path to keep them in their trajectory. Gravitational force, frictional force, and centrifugal acceleration are not the primary forces involved in maintaining the curved flight of the planes.

questions.text: A particle of mass m is executing uniform circular motion on a path of radius r. If P is the magnitude of its linear momentum. The radial force acting on the particle is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option incorrectly combines mass and momentum without considering the relationship with centripetal force.
This option incorrectly combines radius and momentum without considering the relationship with centripetal force.

The correct option. The centripetal force is related to the square of the linear momentum and inversely proportional to the radius.The radial force is the centripetal force that keeps the particle moving in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following formula:Fc = mv2/r where:Fc is the centripetal force (N).m is the mass of the particle (kg).v is the speed of the particle (m/s).r is the radius of the circle (m).We can write the magnitude of the linear momentum as: P = mv. Substituting this into the equation for the centripetal force, we get: Fc =P2/mr. Therefore, the radial force acting on the particle is P2/mr.Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they do not appropriately consider the relationship between linear momentum and centripetal force in uniform circular motion.
This option incorrectly multiplies mass, radius, and momentum without considering the correct formula for centripetal force.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.The radial force is the centripetal force that keeps the particle moving in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following formula:Fc = mv2/r where:Fc is the centripetal force (N).m is the mass of the particle (kg).v is the speed of the particle (m/s).r is the radius of the circle (m).We can write the magnitude of the linear momentum as: P = mv. Substituting this into the equation for the centripetal force, we get: Fc =P2/mr. Therefore, the radial force acting on the particle is P2/mr.Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they do not appropriately consider the relationship between linear momentum and centripetal force in uniform circular motion.

questions.text: A stone to the end of a string 1m is whirled ina horizontal circle with a constant speed. If the stone makes 22 revolution in 44 seconds, What is the magnitude and direction of a acceleration of the stone.
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: π2/4ms-2 and direction along the radius towards the centre
options.explanation: This magnitude is incorrect. The calculation for centripetal acceleration yields π2ms-2, not π2/4ms-2.

options.text: π2ms-2 and direction along the radius towards the centre
options.explanation: a=v²/r = ω²r = 4π²n²r = 4π² (22/44)² × 1 = π²m/s², and its direction is always along the radius and towards the centre.

options.text: π2ms-2 and direction along the radius away from the centre
options.explanation: The magnitude is correct, but the direction is wrong. Centripetal acceleration is an "inward-seeking" force/acceleration, always directed towards the centre of the circle to maintain the circular path. It is never directed away from the centre.

options.text: π2ms-2 and direction along the tangent to the circle
options.explanation: The magnitude is correct, but the direction is wrong. Acceleration along the tangent to the circle is called tangential acceleration. Tangential acceleration occurs only when the speed of the object is changing (either increasing or decreasing). In this problem, the stone is moving with a constant speed, so the tangential acceleration is zero. The only acceleration present is the centripetal acceleration directed towards the centre.

questions.explanation: a=v²/r = ω²r = 4π²n²r = 4π² (22/44)² × 1 = π²m/s², and its direction is always along the radius and towards the centre. In uniform circular motion, the acceleration (centripetal acceleration) is always directed along the radius towards the centre of the circular path.

questions.text: When a body is whirled in a horizontal circle by means of a string, the centripetal force is supplied by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It can be used to measure the centripetal force, but it does not supply centripetal force.

Yes, the centripetal force is supplied by the tension in the string. When a body is whirled in a horizontal circle by means of a string, the string exerts a force on the body towards the centre of the circle. This force is called the centripetal force. The centripetal force is necessary to keep the body moving in a circular path. The tension in the string is the force that the string exerts on the body. The tension in the string is equal to the centripetal force. The tension in the string is given by the following equation:T = Fc = mv2/r

Velocity is related to the kinetic energy and momentum of the body and is a component in the centripetal force formula, but it does not supply the force. Velocity's direction is constantly changing due to the centripetal force.
Centripetal acceleration is the result of the centripetal force acting on the mass, not the source of the force.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Yes, the centripetal force is supplied by the tension in the string. The centripetal force is necessary to keep the body moving in a circular path. The tension in the string is the force that the string exerts on the body. The tension in the string is equal to the centripetal force. The tension in the string is given by the following equation:T = Fc = mv2/r

questions.text: Four particles have the following masses (in terms of m), speeds (in terms of v), and radii (in terms of r). Which particles have the same centripetal force as particle-1?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option describes particle-1 itself, which has a mass of m, speed v, and radius r. Comparing it to itself is redundant, so option A cannot be correct.

Particle-2 has a mass of m/2, speed of 2v, and a radius of 2r. Evaluating the centripetal force: Fc = (m/2 * (2v)2) / (2r) = (4mv2)/(4r) = (mv2)/r, which matches the centripetal force of particle-1. Therefore, option B is correct.
Particle-3 has a mass of 2m, speed of v/2, and the same radius r. The centripetal force is Fc = (2m * (v/2)2) / r = (mv2)/(4r), which is not equal to that of particle-1. Thus, option C is incorrect.

Particle-4 has a mass of m, speed of 2v, and a radius of 3r. Its centripetal force is Fc = (m * (2v)2) / (3r) = (4mv2)/(3r), which does not match particle-1's centripetal force. Thus, option D is incorrect.

Particle-3 and Particle-4 do not match this force due to their respective differences in the mass, speed, and radius combinations, making them incorrect choices.

questions.text: A body travelling in a circular path at constant speed:
questions.subject: Physics

Since in a circular path, the direction of the object is constantly changing, therefore the velocity cannot be constant. A changing velocity means an accelaration is experienced by the body. This accelaration is due to the centripetal and centrifugal forces acting on the body.

questions.text: An object of mass 1 g is whirled in a horizontal circle of radius 0.5m at a constant speed of 2m/s. The work done on the object during one revolution is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The work done is zero because the centripetal force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion, meaning no work is performed.

questions.explanation: The work done on an object is calculated by the product of the force component in the direction of motion and the displacement. In circular motion at constant speed, the centripetal force is always perpendicular to the displacement, resulting in zero work done. This is because work is only done by forces that have a component in the direction of displacement, which is not the case here.Option A is correct because it recognizes that the perpendicular nature of centripetal force results in zero work done. Options B, C, and D incorrectly suggest that non-zero work is done, misunderstanding the role of force direction in work calculation.

questions.text: What is wrong about centripetal and centrifugal forces?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: They act on the same body
options.explanation: Yes, centripetal and centrifugal force can act on the same body. However, they are not the same thing. The centripetal force is the force that keeps an object moving in a circular path. It is always directed towards the center of the circle. The centrifugal force is an apparent force that acts outward on an object moving in a circular path. It is not a real force, but rather a result of the inertia of the object.

No, the centripetal and centrifugal forces do not both perform work.Centripetal force is the force that keeps an object moving in a circular path. It is directed towards the center of the circle. The centripetal force is necessary to overcome the object's inertia, which wants to keep it moving in a straight line. The centripetal force is always perpendicular to the object's velocity.Centrifugal force is an apparent force that acts on an object moving in a circular path. It is directed outward, away from the center of the circle. The centrifugal force is not a real force, but rather a consequence of inertia. The centrifugal force is equal in magnitude to the centripetal force, but it is in the opposite direction.

Yes, the centripetal and centrifugal forces have opposite directions.The centripetal force is the force that keeps an object moving in a circular path. It is directed towards the center of the circle. The centrifugal force is the apparent outward force that an object feels when it is moving in a circular path. It is directed away from the center of the circle. The centripetal force is provided by a real force, such as the tension in a string or the force of gravity. The centrifugal force is a fictitious force that is not actually present. It is only felt by the object because of its inertia.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.No, the centripetal and centrifugal forces do not both perform work.Centripetal force is the force that keeps an object moving in a circular path. It is directed towards the center of the circle. The centripetal force is necessary to overcome the object's inertia, which wants to keep it moving in a straight line. The centripetal force is always perpendicular to the object's velocity.Centrifugal force is an apparent force that acts on an object moving in a circular path. It is directed outward, away from the center of the circle. The centrifugal force is not a real force, but rather a consequence of inertia. The centrifugal force is equal in magnitude to the centripetal force, but it is in the opposite direction.
